title: Chapter 37: Return to the Cottage
content: The cottage was gone.

I stood at the edge of the clearing and tried to make the shapes in front of me mean something else — tried to see the pitched roof, the crooked chimney, the window boxes Nadine had painted green every spring whether they needed it or not. But the chimney had fallen into the hearth, and the roof lay across the floor like a collapsed lung, and the window boxes were ash scattered across stones that had once been a home.
The cottage was gone.
But the garden survived.

It shouldn't have. The fire had swept through the vale with a hunger that respected nothing — not stone, not timber, not the careful wards my grandmother had stitched into every lintel and threshold. By every law of flame and misfortune, the herb garden should have been soot and memory. Instead, it sat in the shadow of ruin, impossibly green, impossibly whole. The rosemary had grown nearly a foot, drunk on whatever the battle had bled into the soil. The thyme had crept past its border, soft and spreading. And Nadine's rose bush — the climber she'd trained along the eastern wall, the one she'd sung to on winter nights — stood there still, burned brown on its left side where a spark had caught, but alive. A single bud, deep red, had opened on the undamaged cane.
I didn't realize I was walking until I was already on my knees.

The soil was warm. Not the thin, reluctant warmth of fog-damp earth — real warmth, the kind that came from a sun that had finally, after two hundred and sixty years, found its way through. I pressed my palms flat against the ground and felt the heat of it sink into my skin, and my eyes burned, and I thought: *oh*. I thought: *I didn't know I'd missed it this much.*
Sunlight hit my face.

I tilted my head back and let it. The fog was lifting — not retreating the way it sometimes did when the wind shifted, not thinning briefly before gathering back like a held breath. It was *ending*. I watched it dissolve over the treeline in great gauzy sheets, peeling away from the hills, releasing the valley floor in stages: first the meadow, then the stream, then the far ridge where the golden barrier still shimmered like a half-remembered dream. The sky above it was blue. Not grey, not the parchment-white I'd mistaken for blue my whole life. *Blue*. The kind of blue that made my chest hurt.

I hadn't known. You can't miss what you've never had, that's what everyone says, but it's a lie. You miss it. You miss it in the part of you that reaches for something without knowing what, in the instinct that turns your face skyward on the rare bright days, in the way your body understands warmth before your mind has a name for it. I had missed the sun my whole life, and I hadn't known it until this moment, kneeling in my grandmother's garden with the light pouring over me like rain after a drought.
A bird sang.

Then another. Then a third, and a fourth, until the clearing filled with a sound I had only ever heard in fragments — true birdsong, uncompressed by fog, undampened by the weight of centuries. They were returning. The birds that had fled the vale, or died in it, or simply gone quiet — they were *back*, and they were singing as if they'd never stopped, as if the world had just been waiting for permission to make noise again.

I breathed in, and the smell hit me — wet soil and rosemary and the green sharp perfume of herbs that had survived fire and magic and the long dull weight of fog. It smelled like the cottage on its best day. It smelled like every morning Nadine had sent me to pick chamomile for tea, every evening Maren's ghost had drifted through the kitchen garden just to run her fingers over the lavender. It smelled like home, which is not a place but a *scent-memory*, and I let it fill me until I could taste it — sunlight on my tongue, warm and golden and impossibly sweet, like honey dissolved in air.
I stayed like that for a long time. Kneeling. Breathing. Letting the world reset around me.
Then I reached into my pocket.

The seeds were there — I'd been carrying them since I'd found them pressed between the pages, tucked into the spines, hidden in the margins of every journal. Maren's lavender, wrapped in a scrap of muslin. Cordelia's sage, tied with kitchen twine. Iris's thyme in a folded letter. Hazel's rosemary in a matchbox. Vivienne's chamomile in a handkerchief she'd probably stolen from some admirer's pocket, because it was monogrammed with initials that weren't hers.
I hadn't planned this. I hadn't thought about it. But my hands knew what to do.

Lavender for Maren. I pressed the seeds into the soil at the garden's edge, where the morning light would reach them first. Maren, who had loved this vale fiercely enough to refuse to leave it — even in death, she'd stayed, tending a garden only she could see. *Rest now*, I thought. *I'll tend it for you.*

Sage for Cordelia. The center of the garden, where the earth was richest. Cordelia, who had written recipes and remedies in a hand so precise it looked printed, who had measured every life in drops and pinches and the careful arithmetic of care. *Rest now.*

Thyme for Iris. Along the stone border, where the roots could find purchase in the cracks. Iris, who had been quiet — the journal between Cordelia's and Hazel's, thinner than the others, filled not with words but with pressed flowers and small sketches and the kind of silence that isn't empty but *full*, too full to speak. *Rest now.*

Rosemary for Hazel. Beside the well, where the ground stayed damp. Hazel, who had been angry — whose journal was half herbalism and half furious prayer, who had written *I will not be the last* on the inside cover in a hand that shook with defiance. *Rest now. You weren't.*

Chamomile for Vivienne. Near the door — or where the door had been. Vivienne, who had been so much braver than anyone gave her credit for, who had woven enchantments into tea cakes and sympathy, who had understood that the deepest magic is the kind no one notices because it feels like love. *Rest now.*

And Nadine's rose bush. I didn't have a seed for Nadine because she didn't need one. Her garden was already here — had *survived*, stubborn and thorned and magnificent, burned on one side but blooming on the other. That was Nadine. That was always Nadine. She had raised me in the space between disaster and stubbornness, and she had taught me that survival isn't persistence — it's *flowering anyway*.

I sat back on my heels and looked at what I'd done. Six plantings. Six ancestresses. And me, kneeling in the soil with dirt under my fingernails and my knees stained brown and my heart cracked open like a seed that had finally found enough light.

I didn't plant a seed for myself. I planted *myself*. Knees in the earth. Hands pressed flat. Rooted. Present. The seventh in the line, not because I'd inherited a title or a duty or a curse, but because I was *here*. Because I chose to stay.
The sunlight moved across the garden, warm on my shoulders, and I let it.
"I figured you'd be here."
I didn't jump. I didn't turn. I just smiled, because some things are so reliably themselves that they don't surprise you — they *ground* you.

Leo stood at the edge of the clearing, hands in his pockets, looking at the ruined cottage with the careful expression of someone who understands that some losses can't be fixed, only witnessed. His gaze moved from the collapsed roof to the surviving garden — to the rose bush, to the fresh-turned soil where I'd planted — and settled on me. Kneeling in the dirt. Probably looked like a mess.
"I brought pie," he said.
"Of course you did."
"Apple."
"Naturally."

Same words. Same rhythm as before — the night I'd broken down in his kitchen, the first time the vale had cracked open and shown me what it really was. But the meaning had shifted. Then, the pie had been comfort. A life raft thrown to someone drowning. Now it was just — pie. Just Leo, being Leo, showing up where I was because that's where he wanted to be.
I got to my feet, brushing soil from my knees. "Give me that before you drop it."
"I've never dropped a pie in my life."
"You dropped two pies. Last month."
"Those were *tactical* drops. There was a raccoon."

I took the pie from his hands — still warm, in a dish that had somehow survived the chaos of the past few days — and set it on the only flat surface left: the garden wall, the section near Nadine's rose bush that had somehow stayed standing. Then I looked at the cottage again. At the debris. The splintered beams. The chimney stones. The bits of life scattered like dropped sentences — a shattered cup, a half-burnt rug, the iron skeleton of the stove.
"We should clear this," I said.
Leo looked at me. "You want to do that now?"
"When else? It's not going to clear itself."
"Literally any time in the future would also work. You survived a magical battle twelve hours ago."
"And now I'm going to do something useful with my hands." I looked at him. "You can watch if you want."
He sighed — the particular sigh of a man who has already lost an argument he hasn't started — and went to find the shovel.

We worked through the morning. Not with magic — I didn't have any left, and even if I had, this wasn't the kind of work that asks for it. This was the slow, physical, bone-deep labor of picking up the pieces of something and deciding what to keep. We moved beams. We stacked stones. I found Nadine's tea service — three cups survived, the fourth was dust — and set them aside. I found the iron hook that had hung over the hearth, blackened but whole. I found a page from Hazel's journal that had blown into the garden and pressed itself against the rosemary, and the rosemary had held it safe.

The sunlight moved across the sky. I hadn't known the sky could hold that much light — hadn't known a whole day could pass without the fog thickening at noon, without the horizon closing in like a slow exhale. I could see the treeline on the far ridge. I could see the clouds — *clouds*, real ones, not fog pretending — drifting across a blue so deep it hurt.

And the birds. They kept coming. A finch landed on the garden wall and watched me work with its head tilted, unbothered. A thrush hopped across the cleared ground and pulled a worm from the soil where I'd planted Maren's lavender, and I let it, because the garden was alive and that meant it belonged to everything — not just me, not just the memory of six women, but the whole waking world.

Leo worked beside me without complaint. He hauled stones to the pile I was building and didn't ask where they were going, because there was no plan yet — just the rhythm of lift and carry, of clearing space so something new could be built. We didn't talk much. We didn't need to. The silence was the kind that comes after a storm, when the world is too stunned to be loud and too relieved to be quiet, and every small sound — the scrape of a shovel, the click of a stone, the far-off call of a bird — feels like a promise.

At some point he handed me a slice of pie and I ate it sitting on the garden wall, dirt still on my hands, and it tasted like apples and cinnamon and butter and *normal*, which was a flavor I hadn't known I was craving.

By late afternoon, the clearing looked different. The rubble was stacked. The garden was raked and weeded and tended. The rose bush stood straighter now — I'd staked the burned cane, and it held, because Nadine's roses had never had the decency to give up. The new plantings were fine lines in the dark soil — invisible promises that would become green in their own time.
And I was tired. The good kind. The kind that comes from using your body for something that matters.

Evening came slowly, the way it does when the sky has space to show off. The sun dropped toward the hills and painted everything gold and rose — the cleared ground, the stacked stones, the surviving garden, the two of us sitting on the wall because it was the only place left to sit. Beyond the meadow, the golden barrier shimmered at the edge of the valley, fainter now but still present. It would take time to fade entirely. That was fine. I could take time.
Leo's hand found mine.

Not the way it had before — not anchoring, not steadying, not the grip of someone trying to keep another person from flying apart. This was different. His fingers slid between mine and stayed there, and there was a question in it, but it wasn't *are you okay* — it was *will you stay*. Not stay here, not stay in the vale — stay *with me*. Stay present. Stay real.
I let him.

I held his hand because I wanted to, and because the wanting was simple and clean and didn't come with the weight of duty or the tremor of fear. I held his hand the way the soil held the seeds — because that's what you do when something matters. You hold on. Not to possess. Just to *be held*.

The last light caught the rose on Nadine's bush and turned it into something luminous — a single red bloom, open and unashamed, lit from behind like a stained-glass window in a church I'd never attended. I looked at it, and I thought about how many things had ended to bring us here, and how many things remained, and how the ratio between them wasn't loss to be mourned but *proof* — proof that what matters survives. Not untouched, not unchanged, but *survives*.

I leaned against Leo's shoulder, and he leaned back, and we sat there as the stars came out — real stars, not the dim smudges I'd grown up with, but sharp bright points of light in a sky that had been waiting two hundred and sixty years to show them off.

Later, when he'd gone to check on the barrier and I was alone with the garden and the quiet, I opened the journal. The one I'd been keeping since the beginning — since Maren's ghost first appeared in the garden and my whole life became a story I needed to write down. I turned to a fresh page, and I wrote:
*The cottage is gone. The garden remains. That's the right ratio.*

*What matters survives. Everything else — the walls, the roof, the careful structure of an ordinary life — those are just the shape we build around what matters. They can fall. They can burn. The green thing at the center holds.*

*I'll rebuild. Not the same cottage — a new one. With better walls and more windows, because I've learned I like the light. With a door that doesn't stick and a hearth that draws clean and a kitchen window that faces east so the sunrise comes in while I make tea.*
*And a garden. Tended by seven generations of women who finally get to rest.*
*I'm the seventh. Not the last — that word belongs to a story that's over. The next word is something else. Something with roots.*
*I'm the seventh, and I'm still here, and the sun came up this morning, and the birds are back, and there are seeds in the ground, and the rose bloomed anyway.*
*That's enough for today. That's more than enough.*

I closed the journal and set it on the wall, and I sat there a while longer with my face turned up to the stars, breathing in the scent of soil and rosemary and the quiet green promise of things not yet grown but already on their way.
